caption: I visited the Okinawa Prefectural Museum of Peace today. It told the awful stories of the battle between the U.S. and Japan for Okinawa in 1945. The civilians of Okinawa suffered from both American and Japanese soldiers during the months long battle. Many hid in caves, tombs, etc. to hide from artillery bombardment and gunfire above ground. Many could not find things to eat or drink, so they had to resort to foraging for tubers, shoots, and what little else they could find. Going outside the caves to relieve themselves was dangerous, so conditions inside caves were often unhygienic. Japanese soldiers entered some caves and took the little food the civilians had by force, or entirely prevented the civilians from staying. In total, over 200,000 lives were lost during the battle for Okinawa. In contrast, wars today that have claimed much fewer lives have been labelled genocides. This photograph near the end of the exhibition stood out to me. It shows  in detail the somber face of this young American soldier kneeling besides the corpse of a Japanese woman. I find myself looking closely at this soldier's face every time I see this photo. I wonder what he was feeling in that moment amidst the horrors around him.

Okinawa has no right looking as gorgeous as it does. I genuinely haven't seen a nicer looking place before. The colour of the water around the island constantly seems unbelievable and filtered. It's like every random corner of the island could be a principal attraction that tourists flock to in other parts of the world. On top of the incredible sights making me happy, I was extremely lucky to have stayed with my host, Thomas's family during my time in Okinawa. I loved their cosy home where I could wake up peacefully to chirping birds and the sunrise. Genuinely the quality of my sleep just reached a different level overnight. It was also nice to just see how the family went about day to day routines in a wholesome way. Everyone seemed happy, calm, relaxed, etc. every day. It made me happy to see that everything was going good for them. I'll have to look back fondly on this time of life, noting how tranquil it's possible for me to be in the right environment.

It's cherry blossom season in Japan. I used to wonder what the point was of having trees that only flower so briefly in the year. Though in the last few days, I've noticed myself much more willing to just stop and admire the blossoms. I'm more willing to wake up early and get out of the house. Generally, I'm more cheerful and productive. It's quite nice to have a beautiful reminder of the current phase of life, knowing that it only lasts so long. It gets me out of the day to day complacency so I can begin again to pay attention to the things that matter.

I've been feeling pretty lonely recently and it's been weighing on me. On the outside, I'm in Japan and I can go to new cities that I probably won't get to go to ever again. Though on the inside, big moments don't necessarily make me any happier than little moments. They say ethics is about doing the right thing when no one is looking. I think happiness is about smiling when no one is looking :-) Today, I found myself smiling to myself more than on some of the 'crazy global roadtrips'. I was just listening to Ocean Eyes by Billie Eilish. I found the harmonies of the song mesmerising, getting wonderfully lost as my attention drifted trying to figure out which pitch to focus on. The video for this song shows Billie Eilish fading in between these flares of light hypnotically, making my attention drift further. I really appreciated that I got to listen to that song before sleeping today. It's been a while since I was able to be so happy about the tiny details of life :-)

Always wanted to check out Japan's dog cafes since my parents never let us get a dog :D The samoyeds I met were characteristic divas that caused mayhem and disrupted this photoshoot :D After chewing my jacket, the one on your right proceeded to stand on my knees for no discernible reason. Still better than cats though :D

Ended up in Hakone this weekend. I wasn't expecting to see Mount Fuji from 32 KILOMETRES away, but it was still the largest feature in the skyline. It must be massive from up close! I guess I see why this mountain is special and so widely depicted. I now have this bizarre urge to go view it from up close.

I visited Kyoto last weekend. Felt pretty restless following around crowds to the big touristy temples. Then, I ran out of cash to enter said touristy temples and took it as divine intervention. Walked North to this local park called Maruyama park where I could just sit alone. While doing that, the sun came out for 30 minutes (only sunshine that day). I just sat, felt the sun warming up my hands, and listened to the gurgles of flowing water in front of this shallow creek. For the first time that day, I felt peaceful instead of restless. Divine intervention successful :D

This was an absolutely epic day. First travel I've ever done with friends. We rented a car and drove around the mountains near Nagano. Everything seemed like a winter wonderland, down to the frost on the branches of the trees. I decided to rent a car and really enjoyed driving around the squiggly mountain roads strewn across the alpine slopes.

I've seen that type of environment in Canada, but I had to drive into the Arctic for days to do so - not hop on a comfortable 2 hour train ride :D Speaking of, the most surreal thing about Japanese shinkansen (rode on the first one today!) is the fact they seem normal. Onboard, you have no clue you're going at 250 km/h because of how quiet, comfortable, and ordinary the experience is.

Tangent aside, we spent most of the day driving to the Jigokudani monkey park. It's full of a bunch of dumb monkeys (like yours truly) taking photos in the cold of a few smart monkeys (who reserved this private onsen for themselves) :D None of us had been so close to such unfazed monkeys in the wild. (All the monkeys in most developing countries try to steal your stuff or attack if you get close.) We left the park quite amazed by all the human like things we saw them doing (kids playing, families huddling, and a lucky few lounging in the onsen).

To wrap up the day, we wandered around a peaceful Shinto shrine in Nagano and reflected on how lucky we were to be young and in this position to see the world unhindered. We left to get dinner at an Indian restaurant that happened to be closed. Luckily, that forced us to stumble into another restaurant nearby that had THE best Indian food I can remember tasting :D The funny thing is that I initially thought the place would be inauthentic since all the signs were in Japanese, the names of the dishes were in Japanese, and the solo proprietor of the restaurant wasn't Indian. To my surprise, the solo proprietor was an absolute rockstar who used all the real ingredients (mustard oil, cardamom, fennel seeds, etc.) that I hadn't seen anywhere else in Japan. My companions found my sudden change of heart extremely funny. Don't go assuming more than you should I guess :D

I went to team labs borderless today. It's this dark maze of interconnected digital art that you have to discover since there's no set path or map. I entered thinking, "Oh, I'll just follow the left wall and solve the maze." I left in awe not knowing or caring where I was and just thinking, "WAIT THERE'S MORE???" In every direction, it was sensory overload :D I'm afraid the title of the best art gallery I've ever been to has been taken away from the Remai Modern in Saskatoon this evening. New side quest unlocked: visit every team labs art installation in Japan :-)

Today morning I woke up at 5 AM on the weekend in a surprisingly well designed capsule inn. Everyone around me was asleep so I thought I was just odd as I improvised a plan to see the sunrise on the beach. Though as I got there at 6:30 AM, these surfers made me feel like I was late to the party. Very punctual Japanese fun :D

I was watching this spy movie where a Soviet spy is captured and faces a harrowing trial where he might be put to death. Though the spy always stays emotionless. The lawyer assigned to him keeps asking, "Are you not worried???" Then the spy always responds, "Would it help?"

So anyways, that's what I did today :D It's been such a bizarre day with the most mind numbing bureaucracies that are not my fault nor in my control. Like surreal levels of pettiness galore just to get 10 words written on a card.

Though getting angry about it changes nothing. So instead, I chose to enjoy dinner with my colleagues Jonathan and Ocean :D Ocean's the MVP of the day and made every challenge we faced an order of magnitude smaller, even though it means he'll have more work to make up for outside working hours. Not the hero we deserve, but the one we need :D I coined our little trio, 'The Three Musketeers' during our many kilometres of walks today. Hopefully more adventures to report amidst us soon :-)

The honest reason I wanted to go to Japan was this scene from the Pixar movie Cars 2. McQueen and Mater arrive in Japan to see incredible neon lights and signs everywhere as they cruise down the streets playing incredible rock vibey music. I wanted to recreate that feeling of wonder I had as a kid in Shinjuku. So while the masses moved around me, I just stood on the corner of the road and listened to that music of my childhood and soaked in the lights. A moment of appreciation while I still have the freedom of youth.

My old boss Wendy once asked me if I felt like a lucky person. I've been thinking about that for a year now and I have to say, I really really do :D Today, my flight to Japan went better than I could have even IMAGINED it going (no literally, there's my conceivable best case and then there's what actually happened).

I met Lyna, who is just an unbelievable person literally pushing forward the boundaries of how incredible people can be :O Lyna has this stunning ability to just make everyone around her feel valued with every tiny joke or compliment or smile. We talked for 3 hours straight about our lives growing up and we went from strangers on a plane to both being in tears. It almost felt scary how much depth and care emanated from her words, and yet she used that immense power and talent seflessly to help a stranger reflect and work towards improving my life.

I'm sorry to rant and I'm genuinely not someone who praises lightly. Though one of my goals in life is to see humans who are the very best at some skill to expand my view of all the wonderful things possible in life. Today, I was lucky enough to have my mind blown in that life-changing way! And to Lyna, I just want to say that I see how you push past personal discomfort and give 110% to be the pillar of support for those around you. It's easy for the rest of us to get complacent and assume you can always be the self-sacrificing superwoman. While I have the privilege to, allow me to say that I recognise and appreciate how hard you work to make a difference in so many lives :-)
